2. Probability Spaces

2.1. Motivation

In discrete settings, it’s easy to define probability as the number of favorable outcomes over total outcomes . In continuous spaces like that logic breaks. You can’t assign uniform probability to every subset of (Vitali paradox). Hence we need to restrict ourself to a collection of “well-behaved” subsets where a consistent definition is possible. Measure theory seeks to explore this.

2.2. Measures

Definition 2 (-field). Given a sample space , a -field is a collection of subsets of , with the following properties:

  2. Closure under complements: If , then in .
  3. Closure under countable unions: If for every , then .

A set is called an event1 or an -measurable set. The pair is called a measurable space.

The intersection of σ-fields is a σ-field. We can use this to construct the “smallest” σ-field containing a collection of subsets of by taking the intersection of all σ-fields containing . There always exists a -field containing a collection of subsets (take for example). This is a well-defined construction and is called the -field generated by , denoted .

If we relax the countable unions property to only finite unions, then we get a field of sets.

Definition 3 (Measure). Let be a measurable space. A measure is a function , which assigns a nonnegative extended real number to every set , and which satisfies the following two conditions:

  1. .
  2. Countable additivity: If is a countable sequence of disjoint sets that belong to , then .

A probability measure is a measure with the additional property that . In that case, the triple is called a probability space.

If an event satisfies , we say that occurs almost surely.

The fact that we use probability measures to represent probability the same thing as the Kolmogorov axioms.

Every measurable space admits a trivial measure. Every measurable space also admits a probability measure: Take any , and let if otherwise 0. This is called a Dirac measure. The problem is that not every measurable space admits a nice probability measure. For example, if we let , there does not exist a uniform measure satisfying (Vitali theorem).

Here is a trivial example of an almost sure event: let and . Then .

Proposition 2 (Properties of probability measures).

  1. Finite additivity.
  2. .
  3. If , then .

  4. Union bound: For any sequence of events, we have

    (3)

  5. PIE / Inclusion-exclusion formula: For any finite collection of events indexed by ,

    (4)

Proof

For (a), pad with empty sets. (b) and (c) are trivial.

We prove (d) by first proving it for finite with induction. It is true for n=2 by PIE. Then we prove the countable case using continuity. In particular, continuity of probability measures (Theorem 2) measures allows the following equality:

(5)

We prove (e) by induction. In the inductive step, distributive the intersection so we get terms and can use the induction hypothesis.

□

To prove a measure satisfies countable addivity, it may be easier to start from finite addivitiy and prove continuity (part d). This is justified by the following theorem:

Theorem 2 (Continuity of probability measures). Let be a -field of subsets of , and suppose that satisfies as well as the finite additivity property. Then, the following are equivalent:

  1. is a probability measure (satisfies countable additivity).
  2. If is an increasing sequence of sets in and , then
  3. If is a decreasing sequence of sets in and , then
  4. If is a decreasing sequence of sets in and is empty, then

2.3. Uniform Distribution: The Lebesgue measure on .

By Vitali’s theorem, we must use a “nicer” -field than to assign a uniform probability on . We will use the Borel σ-field generated by closed intervals in . For example, the set of irrationals is Borel.

Directly defining a probability measure for all Borel sets directly is difficult. We instead begin with an easier collection , consisting of the empty set and finite unions of intervals of the form , and apply the Extension Theorem stated below.

Lemma 1.

  1. The Borel field is generated by , i.e.
  2. The collection is a field of sets.
  3. The collection is not a σ-field.
Proof

□

For every of the form define

(6)

Note that and is finitely additive. We can also prove that is countably additive2. We are ready to apply the Extension Theorem:

Theorem 3 (Carathéodory's Extension Theorem). Let be a field of subsets of a sample space , and let be the σ-field that it generates. Suppose that is a mapping from to that satisfies as well as countable additivity on .

Then can be uniquely extended to a probability measure on .

The intuition is define to be the infimum of over all coverings of from . We can also use the supremum of all packings. The nontrivial step is proving the countable additive prerequisites.

Hence there exists a probabiltiy measure , called the Lesbesgue or uniform measure, defined on the Borel σ-field, that agrees with on . In particular, for every interval .

Example: by complementary counting and countable additivity. By continuity, the probability of a singleton is zero.

Remark. We choose this weird one side open other side closed setup so that is obviously a field (its complement is a union of two similar one side open other side closed intervals. This isn’t true for purely open or closed intervals.) It turns out Borel sets can be defined as generated by open or closed sets or half by continuity.

We extend the uniform measure on to by assigning . Also, from Section 1.1, countable unions of half-open intervals can be used to generate open and closed intervals. Hence and are Borel sets. Hence the same Borel field is generated by open, closed, or half-open intervals on . Working with half-open intervals shows that this Borel field admits a uniform measure.

Assinging probability to intervals on is incompatible with the requirement . We instead only define a measure.

Define the Borel -field of as , where is the collection of intervals in the form . PS2 #3 explores equivalent definitions.

Let be the uniform measure on . Then, define the Lebesgue measure

(7)

We will learn later what Lebesgue integration is.

2.5. Coin Tosses: The Lebesgue measure on

Let . For Vitali-esque technical reasons, we cannot assign a uniform measure to . Uniform here means each possible outcome of the first tosses has the same probability . Hence we choose a subset.

Idea. We create a one-to-one correspondence between and by representing each sequence as the binary representation of a decimal between and . Modulo some technical details, all of the theory we have established for applies here.

We instead begin from scratch. Let be the collection of events whose occurence can be decided by looking at the results of the first tosses, so that . Let

(8)

In PS2, we show (1) is a -field, and (2) is a field but not a -field. For example, let be the event that all tosses are zero. is the event that a one is seen. Neither belong to .

We will define a countably additive measure on and apply the Extension Theorem to obtain a probability measure on . Every set in is of the form for some . Then define . We need to verify consistency when for . and is finitely additive. It turns out is countably additive. By Extension Theorem, there exists a unique probability measure on that assigns equal probability to every possible sequence of length , confirming the process of an infinite sequence of coin can be made rigorous.

2.6. Completion of a Measurable Space

We have defined the Lebesgue measure that acts on the Borel sigma field (). Is there a way to extend it?

Consider a measurable space . Suppose that , and . Any set with this property is called a null set. We can extend the -field by including all subsets of null sets (any such subset clearly requires measure 0).

  1. Let be the collection of all subsets of null sets.
  2. Define , the smallest -field that contains as well as all subsets of null sets.
  3. It turns out we can uniquely extend to by letting for every subset of every null set .

The resulting probability space is said to be complete. All subsets of null sets are included in the -field and are also null sets. When or , is the Borel σ-field, and is the Lebesgue measure, the sets in are called Lebesgue measurable sets. The new measure is also called the Lebesgue measure.

Remarks

  • There exists sets that are Lebesgue measurable but not Borel measurable.
  • There are as many Borel measurable sets as there are points on the real line (cardinality of the continuum), but there are as many Lebesgue measurable sets as there are subsets of the real line (which is a higher cardinality).
  • There exists subsets of that are not Lebesgue measurable.
  • It is not possible to construct a probability space in which the -field includes all subsets of with the probability that for every .

2.7. Vitali Sets and Banach-Tarski paradox.

Ordinary geometric intuition fails when dealing with arbitrary sets.

  1. We will give an example of a non-Lebesgue measurable set (Vitali set) and use it to show that if the Lebesgue measure could assign a length to all subsets of and be translation-invariant, you would get a contradiction. Let stand for addition modulo 1 in . Define and to be equivalent if they differ by a rational number, and partition into equivalence classes. Using axiom of choice, pick one element from each equivalence class and call this set . Consider sets of the form where is a rational. Such sets are translations of each other and disjoint. Suppose is a translation invariant probability measure. Then but this can’t happen since is infinite. We can apply this to by assigning .
  2. Banach-Tarski paradox: let be the 2D surface of the unit sphere in 3D. There exists a subset of such that for any , , where each is a rigid rotation and the sets are disjoint. For example, can be made by three rotated copies of as well as four rotated copies of , suggesting probability 1/3 and 1/4 simultaneously. The full Banach-Tarski paradox says: a solid ball can be decomposed into finitely many disjoint pieces that reassemble into two identical balls using rotations and translations. If we reject axiom of choice (equivalent to saying all vector spaces have a basis), BT paradox disappears!

Definition 5 (Independence). Let be a probability space.

  1. Two events and are said to be independent if . If , an equivalent condition3 is .

  2. Let be a possibly uncountable index set. The events in the family are said to be independent if for every finite subset , we have

    (11)

    This is a stronger definition than pairwise independence. See PS2 #6 for example. We could have an alternate definition for countable subsets .

  3. Let be two -fields. We say and are independent if for any two events and are independent.
  4. More generally, let be an index set of -fields . The -fields are independent if the following holds: if we pick one event from each , the events in the resulting family are independent.

For example, when , and are not independent since knowing guarantees . Similarly when they are not independent.

The following theorem helps us establish that 2 -fields are independent. It turns out we only need to check independence for a smaller collection of sets:

Theorem 4. If and are two collections of measurable sets, that are closed under intersection. If and are independent and, then and are independent.

2.9. Borel-Cantelli

The Borel-Cantelli lemma helps us establish that a certain event has probability zero or one.

Definition 6 (Infinitely Often). Given a sequence of events , the event (read as “ occurs infinitely often”) is defined to be the event consisting of all that belong to infinitely many . Equivalently,

(12)

The limit inferior means “eventually always true”:

(13)

Applying De Morgan’s law twice implies that

(14)

I.e. if never eventually is always true then we must have infinitely many occur.

(15)

I.e. if only finitely many occur then eventually must always occur.

The outer intersection means “for every “ and the inner union means “there exists containing “. In set theory we also denote this as . Hence is the event that infinitely many occur.

Theorem 5 (Borel-Cantelli). Let be a sequence of events and let .

  1. If , then .
  2. If and the events are independent, then .
Proof
  1. implies that . Note that for every , we have . Apply union bound and take the limit as .

  2. Let so that . It suffices to show that for all since then

    (16)

    by union bound. Independence of implies independence of , so we have

    (17)

    The RHS equals zero by the following lemma: Suppose that for every , and that . Then . The lemma is proved by taking the logarithm and showing for . Use the fact that is concave so the graph lies below every tangent line.

□

Example: let be the event that the th coin toss is heads. By lemma 2, the probability that heads occurs infinitely often is 1. Suppose you buy a lottery ticket and your chance of winning is . Then your chance of winning infinitely often is zero.

Example: Let be the event that a random walk is at the origin at time . Borel-Cantelli shows that for , you don’t return to the origin infinitely often.
